Details:
Other processes that have been evaluated for the treatment of per- and polyfluoroalkyl substances (PFASs) in groundwater or at environmentally relevant concentrations (e.g., 1 milligram per liter or less) include electrochemical treatment [2629, 2630], electrocoagulation [2608], plasma treatment [2634], and ultrasonic irradiation [2815, 2816, 2817, 2819, 2825, 2833, 2838, 2845, 2846]. These studies were conducted at the bench scale and did not evaluate the treatment processes for practical use in larger-scale drinking water applications. 
The electrochemical processes [2629, 2630] achieved from less than 0 to greater than 99 percent removal or destruction of the PFASs studied, including perfluorooctanoic acid (PFOA), perfluorooctane sulfonate (PFOS), perfluorohexyl sulfonate (PFHxS), perfluorobutyl sulfonate (PFBS), perfluorobutanoic acid (PFBA), perfluoropentanoic acid (PFPeA), perfluorohexanoic acid (PFHxA), perfluoroheptanoic acid (PFHpA), and perfluoroheptyl sulfonate (PFHpS). These processes were generally more effective with increasing current density and increasing PFAS concentration. 
Electrocoagulation [2608] achieved from 59 to greater than 99 percent removal of or destruction of PFASs including PFOS, PFHxS, and PFBS.
Plasma treatment [2634] achieved 70 percent removal or destruction of PFHxS and greater than 99 percent removal or destruction of PFOS.
Ultrasound treatment [2815, 2816, 2817, 2819, 2825, 2833, 2838, 2845, 2846] varied in effectiveness from 9.5 to 99 percent removal or destruction of the PFASs studied, including PFOA, PFOS, PFHxS, PFBS, PFBA, and PFHxA. These processes were generally more effective with increasing irradiation time and frequency and power inputs.
